A committee adopts the proposal and begins sorting candidate explanations through layered filters. Early stages reward grammar and coherence. The result is immediate: the number of candidates drops sharply. Most outputs now read cleanly, fit prevailing standards, and can be quoted without embarrassment.

But the committee also accepts the historical caution built into the premises. Some ideas later judged important first appeared incoherent or low-quality by those same standards. So the success of the filter stack is unstable. If it removes what looks bad too early, it may also remove the very region where rare explanatory novelty appears. The cleaner the first pass, the greater the danger that the surviving set is dominated by cliché.

To test whether the architecture can recover such novelty, the committee turns to the Bern patent office story. That setting plainly matters. It reminds them that fruitful problems can be shaped by nonlinguistic constraints, by institutional position, and by selective attention. Yet the architecture does not obviously represent those factors. It processes linguistic form and apparent coherence, but not what it is like to be placed where certain problems become salient and others invisible. Therefore, even if the stack produces polished explanations, it may still miss the conditions that generated the valuable problem choice in the first place.

Inside this tension, ordinary operations continue. A sales clerk notes that a delay in billing can make a quoted customer choke. This is not a metaphor in the system; it is one more practical constraint. Timing affects uptake. An explanation may be acceptable in form yet fail in reception because the surrounding transaction is badly timed. That reinforces the committee’s worry: quality is not exhausted by textual filters if external conditions alter whether the result can even be borne.

Nearby, a woman utters orthodox prayers while focusing on orchids in the crock. Her behavior is legible to the committee’s framework only in part. The prayers are orthodox, so they pass a conventional linguistic and doctrinal filter. But her attention is elsewhere, fixed on the orchids. The scene separates surface conformity from the actual structure of attention. This gives the committee a concrete analogue of the architecture’s limitation: what passes the early filter may not reveal what is doing the real cognitive work.

The world implied by all this is one in which layered evaluation can efficiently enforce recognizable quality, yet risks selecting for the familiar. Historically important novelty may begin by failing exactly those filters. Settings like Bern matter because institutions, constraints, and attention help generate problems, but the architecture does not clearly model them. Practical timing can distort reception, as with delayed billing and the choking customer. And orthodox utterance can coexist with attention elsewhere, as with the woman, the prayers, and the orchids in the crock. So the proposal’s filters organize appearances of explanation better than they explain the emergence of rare, fruitful explanation.
